title: Your First Close Read: Reading Like a Thinker
description: Practice reading a short non-fiction text with active strategies. This session builds comprehension, vocabulary, and critical reading skills simultaneously.

steps.instruction: Find a short news article or informational paragraph (5-10 sentences). Before reading, look at the title, headings, and any images. Read about main idea: what clues do titles and headings give about the main idea?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can make a prediction about the main idea before reading based on visual and textual clues
steps.hint: Titles and headings are written to summarize — they deliberately signal the main topic

steps.instruction: Read the text once through. Circle or underline any words you do not know. Read about word learning strategies: how can you figure out an unknown word's meaning from context?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can use surrounding context — adjacent sentences, examples, contrasts — to infer the meaning of unknown words
steps.hint: Look at the sentence before and after the unknown word — authors often define or exemplify terms they introduce

steps.instruction: Re-read the text and identify: What is explicitly stated? What must you infer? Read about inferencing: what information does the author imply without stating directly?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can distinguish literal statements from implied meanings and draw at least one valid inference from the text
steps.hint: Inferences go beyond what is written — they connect stated facts to unstated conclusions using logic

steps.instruction: Identify how the text is organized: Is it cause-effect? Compare-contrast? Problem-solution? Chronological? Read about text structure: why does recognizing structure help comprehension?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can name the dominant text structure and explain how it helps the author communicate their message
steps.hint: Signal words reveal structure: 'because' and 'therefore' = cause-effect; 'however' and 'on the other hand' = compare-contrast

steps.instruction: Write a 2-sentence summary of the text. Then ask: Why did the author write this? What is their purpose and perspective? Read about author purpose and perspective.
steps.expectedOutcome: You can write a concise summary and identify whether the author's purpose is to inform, persuade, or entertain, and what perspective they take
steps.hint: Summary captures main idea + key details only; author purpose asks what effect the author intended to have on the reader
